activities_projects.title: Lustre and Appearance Observation
activities_projects.objective: Observe the metallic shine of fresh surfaces
steps: Observe the initial appearance of the metal samples
Rub the surface of each metal with sandpaper for a few seconds
Note the change in the appearance and shine of the surface
Compare the shine of a freshly cleaned surface to an uncleaned one
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies that metals in their pure state have a shining surface, known as metallic lustre
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct observation of the brightness of the surface after sanding (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Testing Malleability
activities_projects.objective: Understand the property of metals to be beaten into sheets
steps: Place the iron nail on a hard surface and strike it with a hammer several times
Repeat the same process with the piece of coal and the zinc granule
Observe which substances change shape and which ones break into pieces
Record which materials are 'malleable'
activities_projects.evaluation: Accurate identification of malleable vs. brittle substances (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Ductility Demonstration
activities_projects.objective: Recognize the ability of metals to be drawn into wires
steps: Examine the copper and aluminum wires in your surroundings (e.g., electrical cables)
Try to find if sulfur or coal can be stretched into thin wires
List metals that are commonly used to make wires
Identify the most ductile metal (Gold) from the textbook
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that ductility is a unique physical property of metals
activities_projects.evaluation: Listing at least three metals used for making wires and naming the most ductile metal (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Heat Conductivity Test
activities_projects.objective: Observe how metals conduct thermal energy
steps: Clamp the metal rod to a stand
Fix a pin to the free end of the rod using wax
Heat the rod with a burner at the clamped end
Observe what happens to the pin after some time
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Concludes that metals are good conductors of heat and have high melting points
activities_projects.evaluation: Explanation of why the pin fell off (melting of wax due to heat transfer) (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Electrical Conductivity Circuit
activities_projects.objective: Test which materials allow electricity to pass through
steps: Set up a simple electric circuit with a bulb and battery, leaving a gap between two clips
Insert a metal key between the clips and see if the bulb glows
Repeat the test with a rubber eraser
Note down which material is a conductor and which is an insulator
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies that metals are generally good conductors of electricity
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct classification of the tested materials as conductors or insulators (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Sonority Check
activities_projects.objective: Observe the sound produced by metals on striking
steps: Strike the steel spoon against a hard surface and listen to the sound
Strike the wooden scale and the plastic bottle against the same surface
Compare the 'ringing' sound of the metal with the 'dull' sound of the non-metals
Discuss why school bells are made of metal
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Defines sonority as the property of metals to produce a ringing sound
activities_projects.evaluation: Ability to describe the difference between sounds produced by metals and non-metals (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Nature of Metal Oxides
activities_projects.objective: Test if metal oxides are acidic or basic
steps: Burn a magnesium ribbon and collect the ash (Magnesium Oxide)
Dissolve the ash in a small amount of water
Test the resulting solution with red and blue litmus paper
Observe which litmus paper changes color
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Learns that most metal oxides are basic in nature and turn red litmus blue
activities_projects.evaluation: Recording the correct color change for the litmus test (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Reaction of Metals with Water
activities_projects.objective: Observe the reactivity of different metals with water
materials: Small piece of Sodium (under teacher supervision)
steps: Take a beaker half-filled with water
Carefully place a small, dried piece of sodium in the water
Observe the reaction (keep distance)
Drop an iron nail in another beaker of water and observe if any immediate change occurs
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that some metals react vigorously with water while others react very slowly
activities_projects.evaluation: Describing the vigor of the sodium reaction vs. the iron reaction (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Reaction with Dilute Acids
activities_projects.objective: Check for the evolution of Hydrogen gas
steps: Take a small piece of aluminum foil in a test tube
Add 2ml of dilute HCl to the test tube
Observe the bubbles of gas being formed
Bring a burning matchstick to the mouth of the tube and listen for a 'pop' sound
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies that metals react with acids to produce a salt and hydrogen gas
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct identification of the 'pop' sound as a test for Hydrogen gas (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Displacement Observation
activities_projects.objective: Observe a simple displacement reaction in daily life
steps: Dissolve some copper sulphate in water to get a blue solution
Drop a clean iron nail into the solution
Wait for 30 minutes and observe the color of the solution
Look at the surface of the iron nail for any brown coating
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that a more reactive metal (Iron) can displace a less reactive metal (Copper)
activities_projects.evaluation: Description of the color change from blue to green (10 marks)
